@graph.headline: Charitable Contributions of Cryptocurrency: Why the IRS Requires a Qualified Appraisal Even When Exchange Prices Exist
@graph.description: The IRS requires a qualified appraisal for cryptocurrency donations exceeding $5,000—even though crypto trades on exchanges with real-time prices. Here is what you need to know to protect your charitable deduction.

mainEntity.name: Do I need an appraisal to deduct a cryptocurrency donation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Under IRS Memo 202302012, a qualified appraisal is required for crypto donations exceeding $5,000, even though crypto trades on exchanges with readily available prices. Cryptocurrency is not classified as 'readily valued property' under IRC §170.

mainEntity.name: Can I use the exchange price for a crypto charitable donation?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. The IRS has explicitly stated that cryptocurrency is not 'readily valued property' for purposes of §170. An exchange price does not substitute for a qualified appraisal.

mainEntity.name: What form do I file for crypto charitable donations?

acceptedAnswer.text: IRS Form 8283 (Noncash Charitable Contributions) must be attached to your tax return for donations exceeding $5,000. For donations of $500,000 or more, the full appraisal report must be attached.

mainEntity.name: What happens if I don't get a qualified appraisal?

acceptedAnswer.text: The IRS will likely disallow the entire deduction. Gibson v. Commissioner (2026) resulted in a $188,563 deduction being fully denied due to substantiation failures.

@graph.name: Qualified Digital Asset Valuations (QDAV)
@graph.description: USPAP-compliant, IRS-qualified appraisals for cryptocurrencies, NFTs, and complex digital assets for tax, estate, and charitable contribution purposes.

@graph.name: Qualified Cryptocurrency Appraisal
@graph.description: USPAP-compliant qualified appraisals for cryptocurrency charitable contributions, satisfying IRC §170(f)(11)(C) requirements.
